paraphrase: The chapter's tone and imagery are unusually dark and ominous, filled with dead men, dark clouds, blood stew, crows, chambers of bones, a talking corpse, and whispers in the dark, setting the scene for something bad to happen.
verbatim: The moon was a crescent, thin and sharp as the blade of a knife. A pale sun rose and set and rose again. Red leaves whispered in the wind. Dark clouds filled the skies and turned to storms. Lightning flashed and thunder rumbled, and dead men with black hands and bright blue eyes shuffled round a cleft in the hillside but could not enter. Under the hill, the broken boy sat upon a weirwood throne, listening to whispers in the dark as ravens walked up and down his arms.

spoken_quote: The moon was a crescent, thin and sharp as the blade of a knife
paraphrase: The line about the moon being a thin, knife-sharp crescent recurs four times in this chapter, a repeated emphasis on knives, flesh, and blood used to track the passage of time.

spoken_quote: thick and heavy, with dark red veins running through it
paraphrase: The bowl of weirwood-seed paste Bloodraven and the children of the forest give Bran is described as thick and heavy, with dark red veins running through it.
verbatim: “The trees will teach him,” said Leaf. She beckoned, and another of the singers padded forward, the white-haired one that Meera had named Snowylocks. She had a weirwood bowl in her hands, carved with a dozen faces, like the ones the heart trees wore. Inside was a white paste, thick and heavy, with dark red veins running through it. “You must eat of this,” said Leaf. She handed Bran a wooden spoon.

paraphrase: The dark red veins running through the paste look remarkably like blood.
verbatim: Something about the look of it made Bran feel ill. The red veins were only weirwood sap, he supposed, but in the torchlight they looked remarkably like blood. He dipped the spoon into the paste, then hesitated. “Will this make me a greenseer?”

paraphrase: The stated purpose of the paste is to help awaken Bran's gifts and wed him to the trees.
Bran eats the paste and has a vision through the eyes of a weirwood tree; immediately afterward, he finds Jojen and Meera curiously absent, and that chapter is the last we see of Bran in the books.
While Bran trains, Jojen mopes around Bloodraven's cave, growing ever more sullen and solitary.
verbatim: The moon was a black hole in the sky. Outside the cave the world went on. Outside the cave the sun rose and set, the moon turned, the cold winds howled. Under the hill, Jojen Reed grew ever more sullen and solitary, to his sister’s distress. She would often sit with Bran beside their little fire, talking of everything and nothing, petting Summer where he slept between them, whilst her brother wandered the caverns by himself. Jojen had even taken to climbing up to the cave’s mouth when the day was bright. He would stand there for hours, looking out over the forest, wrapped in furs yet shivering all the same.

spoken_quote: sadder now, sullen, with a weary, haunted look about the eyes
paraphrase: Even though food and rest improve Jojen's physical health after the journey, he seems sadder, sullen, with a weary, haunted look about the eyes.
verbatim: “The secrets of the old gods,” said Jojen Reed. Food and fire and rest had helped restore him after the ordeals of their journey, but he seemed sadder now, sullen, with a weary, haunted look about the eyes. “Truths the First Men knew, forgotten now in Winterfell … but not in the wet wild. We live closer to the green in our bogs and crannogs, and we remember. Earth and water, soil and stone, oaks and elms and willows, they were here before us all and will still remain when we are gone.”

paraphrase: At one point Jojen implies that he has reason to be afraid.

spoken_quote: will not even try and fight his fate. He says the greendreams do not lie
paraphrase: Meera says Jojen will not even try to fight his fate, because he says the greendreams do not lie — implying Jojen has resigned himself to something his prophetic dreams have shown him.
verbatim: “He wants to go home,” Meera told Bran. “He will not even try and fight his fate. He says the greendreams do not lie.”

paraphrase: Jojen has hinted elsewhere that he has foreseen his own death in his greendreams.
Magic in this world always has a price: when Daenerys uses blood magic to try to save Drogo, the price is a horse's life and her unborn child.
Melisandre needs king's blood and the burning of innocents to work her most powerful magic.
Euron Greyjoy makes blood sacrifices to make his ships sail faster, another example of blood magic requiring a price.

We are told repeatedly that northmen used to make human sacrifices to the weirwoods, and the Skagosi reportedly still do, supporting the idea that the magic of the old gods specifically requires human sacrifice.

Bran's last vision in the same chapter is of a woman slashing a captive's throat before a weirwood tree, and as the man's life flows out of him in a red tide, Bran can taste the blood — directly juxtaposing the paste-eating scene with old-gods human sacrifice imagery.

In blood magic, one person's blood can be more powerful than another's (e.g. Melisandre specifically needs king's blood), which could explain why Jojen's blood specifically would be needed: although not a fully-fledged greenseer like Bloodraven, Jojen has green dreams and distinctive moss-green eyes marking his gift, so his blood carries the power of the old gods.
Cannibalism is a recurring theme in the north: Stannis's starving soldiers eat their own fallen.
Wyman Manderley bakes Freys into pies and serves them, another example of cannibalism in the story.
Varamyr eats human flesh while warged into his wolf, another example of cannibalism in the story.

Cannibalism, especially unknowing cannibalism, is specifically a recurring theme in Bran's own chapters: at the Nightfort, Bran recalls the tale of the Rat Cook, who cooked an Andal prince in a pie and served him to his own father, then was cursed to live as a rat eating his own young.
In the cave of the three-eyed crow, Bran and the others eat suspicious meat that might be squirrel, or rat, or maybe something else.
On the journey north, Coldhands feeds Bran the flesh of some Night's Watchmen and tells him it's pork — an instance of Bran already unknowingly eating human flesh on this same journey.
Like Varamyr, Bran has already eaten human flesh while warged into his direwolf, establishing that Bran has a precedent of (unknowing) cannibalism.

On the journey north, Bran and company eat Coldhands' elk, which Bran feels guilty about because he considers the elk a friend — so Bran has already eaten someone he cared about, not just human flesh in general.
Bloodraven is a hundred-and-twenty-five-year-old figure described as half-corpse and half-tree, undercutting any image of him as a benevolent mentor.
verbatim: Seated on his throne of roots in the great cavern, half-corpse and half-tree, Lord Brynden seemed less a man than some ghastly statue made of twisted wood, old bone, and rotted wool. The only thing that looked alive in the pale ruin that was his face was his one red eye, burning like the last coal in a dead fire, surrounded by twisted roots and tatters of leathery white skin hanging off a yellowed skull.

spoken_quote: in and out of his withered flesh
paraphrase: Weirwood roots are described as snaking in and out of Bloodraven's withered flesh, reinforcing his monstrous, unnatural condition.
verbatim: The sight of him still frightened Bran—the weirwood roots snaking in and out of his withered flesh, the mushrooms sprouting from his cheeks, the white wooden worm that grew from the socket where one eye had been. He liked it better when the torches were put out. In the dark he could pretend that it was the three-eyed crow who whispered to him and not some grisly talking corpse.

spoken_quote: less a man than some ghastly statue made of twisted wood, old bone, and rotted wool
paraphrase: Bloodraven is described as less a man than some ghastly statue made of twisted wood, old bone, and rotted wool.
verbatim: Seated on his throne of roots in the great cavern, half-corpse and half-tree, Lord Brynden seemed less a man than some ghastly statue made of twisted wood, old bone, and rotted wool. The only thing that looked alive in the pale ruin that was his face was his one red eye, burning like the last coal in a dead fire, surrounded by twisted roots and tatters of leathery white skin hanging off a yellowed skull.

paraphrase: Bloodraven's cave is littered with the bones of thousands dead, showing his home is a site of mass death.
verbatim: The caves were timeless, vast, silent. They were home to more than three score living singers and the bones of thousands dead, and extended far below the hollow hill. “Men should not go wandering in this place,” Leaf warned them. “The river you hear is swift and black, and flows down and down to a sunless sea. And there are passages that go even deeper, bottomless pits and sudden shafts, forgotten ways that lead to the very center of the earth. Even my people have not explored them all, and we have lived here for a thousand thousand of your man-years.”

paraphrase: Bloodraven tells Bran not to fear the darkness, because darkness will make him strong — dark, seductive, manipulative mentorship rhetoric more like Vader or the Emperor than Yoda.
verbatim: There he sat, listening to the hoarse whispers of his teacher. “Never fear the darkness, Bran.” The lord’s words were accompanied by a faint rustling of wood and leaf, a slight twisting of his head. “The strongest trees are rooted in the dark places of the earth. Darkness will be your cloak, your shield, your mother’s milk. Darkness will make you strong.”

spoken_quote: a wise old wizard who could fix his legs
paraphrase: Bran had expected Bloodraven to be a wise old wizard who could fix his legs; he never signed up to become a psychic tree, underscoring how Bran was misled about what he was getting into.
verbatim: What was he now? Only Bran the broken boy, Brandon of House Stark, prince of a lost kingdom, lord of a burned castle, heir to ruins. He had thought the three-eyed crow would be a sorcerer, a wise old wizard who could fix his legs, but that was some stupid child’s dream, he realized now. I am too old for such fancies, he told himself. A thousand eyes, a hundred skins, wisdom deep as the roots of ancient trees. That was as good as being a knight. Almost as good, anyway.

paraphrase: Even if Leaf claims Bloodraven works for the greater good, he risks the group's lives bringing them to his cave, never explains what he actually wants, and is turning Bran into something Bran never wanted to become — if sacrificing Jojen served his goals, it's easy to believe he'd do it.

spoken_quote: For us, for you, for the realms of men
paraphrase: Leaf claims the children of the forest and Bloodraven work for us, for the humans, and for the realms of men — a claimed benevolent motive that the video treats as unproven and possibly self-serving cover.
verbatim: “Most of him has gone into the tree,” explained the singer Meera called Leaf. “He has lived beyond his mortal span, and yet he lingers. For us, for you, for the realms of men. Only a little strength remains in his flesh. He has a thousand eyes and one, but there is much to watch. One day you will know.”

paraphrase: This parallels Sansa's arc: she dreamed of being a noble lady and got what she wished for, only to discover Joffrey is a monster and Cersei a vile, scheming, evil bitch — a wished-for dream turning out to be horrifying.
verbatim: “To help me to my grave. I asked for you to leave. Will you make me call my gaolers and have you dragged away, you vile, scheming, evil bitch?”

paraphrase: Bran is starting to make a similar realization to Sansa's: Coldhands is a monster, and the mystical three-eyed crow he traveled so far to find is actually a creepy half-corpse tree-man — setting up a parallel disillusionment arc that could culminate in the horrific discovery that he ate his friend.

Bran has already done messed-up things, like repeatedly warging into Hodor even though it clearly distresses Hodor, suggesting Bran's arc is already trending toward embracing the darkness rather than resisting it.

In the TV show, Jojen is already dead by this point in the story: he is stabbed, then mercy-killed, then blown up by a child of the forest, with no cannibalism or paste element involved — the theory only applies to the book continuity, since the show resolved Jojen's fate differently.
The video explicitly states there is no strong direct evidence that Bran eats Jojen — though it also notes there is no strong evidence against it either.
